items.html:
The human cost of a fall

Falls are one of the most common causes of injury among older adults, and their effects reach far beyond the moment someone hits the floor. A fall can cause a fracture — a broken hip is especially serious — a head injury, bruising, or a long time on the floor before help arrives. The physical harm, though, is only part of the story. After a fall many people lose confidence in their own movement and become frightened of falling again. This is often called a ‘fear of falling’. Understandably cautious, they may start to avoid walking, stairs or going out. Sadly this can make matters worse: muscles weaken, balance declines and the risk of another fall actually rises. A single fall can be the start of a decline in a person’s independence, mood and quality of life.

Your legal duty

Keeping people safe from falls is not only good care; it is the law. The Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974 places a duty on your employer to protect the health and safety of both workers and the people you support, so far as is reasonably practicable. The Health and Safety Executive (HSE) is the regulator that enforces this. On top of this, the Care Act 2014 makes promoting a person’s wellbeing central to good care, and your own duty of care means acting to prevent avoidable harm. Certain serious falls connected with work may have to be reported under RIDDOR. None of this means wrapping people in cotton wool. The aim is to balance safety with a person’s right to move, take everyday risks and stay independent, which is why falls prevention is a shared responsibility across the whole team.

items.question: A person you support has become frightened of falling after a recent trip and now avoids walking around their own home. Why is this fear itself a concern?

answers.text: Avoiding activity weakens the muscles and worsens balance, which can actually increase the risk of another fall.
answers.feedback: Correct. Fear of falling often leads people to move less, and inactivity reduces strength and balance — making a further fall more, not less, likely.

answers.text: It is not a concern; sitting still is the safest way for them to avoid ever falling again.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Becoming inactive weakens the body and raises the risk of falling, as well as harming independence and mood.

answers.text: Fear of falling only matters if the person was actually injured in the first fall.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. The fear affects a person whether or not they were hurt, and it can trigger a decline in confidence and mobility either way.

answers.text: There is nothing you can do about it, so the fear is best ignored.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. You can help by gently encouraging safe activity, building confidence and arranging support, so the fear should never be ignored.

items.html:
Two kinds of risk factor

Why one person falls and another does not usually comes down to a combination of factors. It helps to split these into two groups. Intrinsic factors come from within the person themselves:

  • Medication: some medicines, and especially several taken together, can cause drowsiness, dizziness or low blood pressure.
  • Eyesight: poor or uncorrected vision makes hazards hard to spot.
  • Continence: needing the toilet urgently, or often at night, leads to rushing.
  • Health conditions: dizziness, arthritis, a previous stroke, foot problems or confusion all affect stability.

Muscle weakness and poor balance also belong in this group, and a person who has already fallen is at greater risk of falling again.

Extrinsic factors and joined-up assessment

Extrinsic factors come from the environment and the things around the person:

  • Lighting: dim or glaring light hides trip hazards, especially on stairs and at night.
  • Footwear: loose slippers, worn soles or walking in socks give little grip or support.
  • Environment: clutter, trailing cables, loose rugs, wet floors and missing handrails.
  • Walking aids: a frame or stick of the wrong height, or left out of reach.

Because falls usually have several causes at once, good practice — recommended by NICE — is a multifactorial assessment. This considers all of a person’s risk factors together, intrinsic and extrinsic, and then puts in place a set of actions tailored to that individual. Tackling just one cause on its own rarely works; you address the whole picture.

items.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap to complete the summary of falls risk factors.
items.textField: Risk factors that come from within the person, such as *medication* side effects or poor eyesight, are called *intrinsic* factors. Those that come from the surroundings, such as poor *lighting* or a loose rug, are called *extrinsic* factors. Because a fall usually has several causes at once, NICE recommends a *multifactorial* assessment that considers them all together.

items.html:
Making the environment safer

Small changes to a person’s surroundings can prevent many falls. Look at where someone walks, stands and moves most, and make those places as safe as you can.

  • Handrails and grab rails: secure rails on stairs, and grab rails by the toilet, bath and bed, give people something firm to hold. They must be fitted properly by a competent person.
  • Flooring: non-slip flooring and firmly fixed, well-fitting floor coverings reduce slips; remove or tape down loose rugs and mats.
  • Lighting: good, even lighting, including a night light on the route to the toilet, helps people see hazards.
  • Clear routes: keep walkways free of clutter, trailing cables and spills.

Equipment that supports safe movement

The right equipment helps a person move confidently and independently.

  • Walking aids: sticks, frames and rollators must be the correct height and in good repair, and kept within the person’s reach.
  • Footwear: well-fitting, supportive shoes or slippers with a firm, non-slip sole are far safer than loose slippers or socks.
  • Sensor mats and alarms: pressure mats or falls sensors can alert you when someone at high risk is getting up, so you can offer help in time.
  • Hip protectors: padded garments that cushion the hip may be suggested for some people at high risk of fracture, as part of a wider plan.

Equipment should always be recommended through assessment — often by an occupational therapist or physiotherapist — and you should only ever use it as you have been trained and as set out in the person’s care plan.

items.question: Sensor or pressure mats are used so that staff can restrain a person and stop them getting up on their own.
items.correctMsg: Correct — this is false. Sensor mats are not a means of restraint. They simply alert staff that someone at high risk is getting up, so help can be offered in time while the person keeps their freedom to move.
items.wrongMsg: Not quite. This is false. Sensor mats do not restrain anyone; they alert staff so support can be offered promptly, while respecting the person’s freedom to move.

items.html:
Why staying active protects against falls

One of the most effective ways to prevent falls is often the most overlooked: keeping people moving. Muscle strength and good balance are what keep us steady, and both fade quickly when a person stops using them. When someone becomes less active — often because they are afraid of falling — their legs weaken and their balance worsens, making a fall more likely rather than less. Encouraging safe, regular activity helps break this cycle. This does not mean strenuous exercise. Everyday movement counts: standing up from a chair without using the arms where it is safe to do so, walking a little further, and gentle strength and balance exercises. NICE recommends strength and balance training for older people at risk of falling, as it is one of the few measures shown to make a real difference.

Falls services and your role

You are not expected to prescribe exercises yourself, but you have an important part to play, and specialist help is available.

  • Physiotherapists: assess movement and set safe, individual strength and balance programmes.
  • Falls services or clinics: many areas have a dedicated team, often reached through the GP, who carry out a full assessment for people who fall repeatedly.
  • Occupational therapists: advise on equipment and on adapting the home.

Your role is to notice when someone is at risk or has started to fall, to raise it so a referral can be made, and then to encourage and support the exercises and safe mobility that are recommended. Praise effort, build confidence, and never rush a person or do for them what they can safely do for themselves.

items.question: Mrs Okafor has become nervous about walking since a minor fall and now spends almost all day sitting down. How can you best help reduce her risk of falling?

answers.text: Gently encourage safe activity and support any strength and balance exercises recommended for her, raising a referral if she needs one.
answers.feedback: Correct. Keeping her safely active maintains strength and balance, and involving physiotherapy or a falls service gives her a programme suited to her needs.

answers.text: Encourage her to stay seated as much as possible, since sitting still removes the risk of falling.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Inactivity weakens muscles and worsens balance, which increases the risk of falling and reduces her independence.

answers.text: Do everything for her so that she never needs to stand or walk at all.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Taking over robs her of movement she needs to stay strong and steady; the aim is to support safe activity, not remove it.

answers.text: Tell her the fall was her own fault so that she is more careful in future.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Blame damages confidence and trust; she needs encouragement and practical support, not criticism.

items.html:
What to check when someone has fallen

If you find someone on the floor, stay calm — your response can prevent further harm. Do not rush to lift them. First, check the situation.

  • Response and breathing: are they conscious and breathing normally? If they are unresponsive or not breathing normally, call 999 and start your emergency procedure.
  • Injury and pain: ask where it hurts and look for signs of a fracture, such as a shortened or turned-out leg, or an obvious deformity.
  • What happened: if they can tell you, find out whether they felt dizzy, blacked out or hit their head.
  • Comfort: keep them warm and reassured while you decide what to do.

Never assume a fall is minor just because the person says they are fine.

When to call 999, and never lifting by hand

Call 999 for an ambulance if any of these apply, even if the person plays it down:

  • Head injury: they hit their head, or seem drowsy, confused or unwell afterwards.
  • Anticoagulants: they take blood-thinning medicines such as warfarin, apixaban or rivaroxaban, as these can cause dangerous bleeding, including inside the head, after a knock.
  • Suspected fracture: severe pain, an inability to move or bear weight, or a limb that looks wrong.
  • Unresponsive, unwell, or you are simply unsure.

Crucially, if someone may be injured, do not move them, and never lift them from the floor by hand. Manual lifting risks worsening a fracture or head injury and can injure you too. If it is safe to raise an uninjured person, use suitable equipment, such as a raising cushion or hoist, as you have been trained. Then record and report the fall so its cause can be reviewed.

items.question: You find Mr Adeyemi on his bedroom floor. He says he simply slipped and feels fine, but he takes apixaban and has a small graze on his forehead. What should you do?

answers.text: Do not lift him; keep him still and warm, and call 999, because a knock to the head while on blood-thinning medication can cause dangerous bleeding.
answers.feedback: Correct. A head injury in someone taking an anticoagulant is an emergency even if they feel well; he must not be moved and needs urgent medical assessment.

answers.text: Help him straight up off the floor, since he says he feels fine and the graze looks small.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. He has hit his head while on a blood thinner; lifting him and assuming he is fine could miss a serious, hidden bleed.

answers.text: Give him a cup of tea and note it in the diary, as there is no need to involve anyone else.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. This situation needs a 999 call, not just a diary note; delaying could let a dangerous bleed go untreated.

answers.text: Wait a few hours to see whether he develops a headache before deciding whether to call for help.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Waiting is dangerous with a head injury on anticoagulants; you should call 999 straight away rather than watch and hope.
